title: Programming and Downloading
text:
Course home

Module 2


Programming and Downloading


Move from study design to careful device initialization, complete download records, and traceable raw data handoff.


ActiLife setup Download logs Raw vs epoch files






Learning outcomes


By the end of this module, you should be able to:


Initialize an ActiGraph device using a documented protocol.
Download recordings into reproducible folders and separate complete from incomplete collections.
Explain the difference between raw .gt3x files and epoch-level .agd files.
Record valid-day rules before data processing begins.



2.1 Before initialization 2.2 Initialization workflow 2.3 Download workflow 2.4 Raw files and epochs



Lesson 2.1


Before Initialization

Before initializing a device, confirm the study identifier, device identifier, planned wear location, start and stop schedule, sampling settings, participant instructions, and the operator responsible for programming. Use a pre-deployment checklist so each device is initialized consistently.
Valid day means a participant-day that meets the study’s minimum data rule. In this course example, a valid participant record requires ≥4 valid days, ≥10 hours of awake wear per valid day, and at least 1 weekend day. These rules are common in physical-activity epidemiology, but they must be approved for each study population, protocol, and placement.

Protocol review required: Sampling frequency, start/stop times, participant identifiers, and initialization settings must come from the approved study protocol. Do not reuse settings from another cohort without review.




Lesson 2.2


ActiLife Initialization Workflow

If the initialization click path will be shown in the video, keep the webpage short and use this as a checklist. If the learner is working from the webpage alone, follow the steps below:

Open ActiLife and connect the device.
Select Initialize.
Choose Regular Initialization.
Enter Subject Info using the study-approved participant ID and device assignment.
Use clear only when the protocol confirms the device should be reset before deployment.
Click Initialize 1 device.
Record the initialization date, operator, device serial number, participant ID, planned wear period, and any issues in the deployment log.


示例: Pelotas/UFPel cohort Use ActiGraph as the running example. Participant IDs, device serial numbers, start/stop dates, wear location, and operator initials should be logged before monitors leave the lab. This example illustrates a reproducible workflow; your own study must decide placement, sampling frequency, wear duration, and valid-day rules before initialization.


Video 2.1 — Programming a device in ActiLife (target: 6–8 minutes) Demonstrate the click path: Initialize -> Regular Initialization -> Enter Subject Info -> clear when approved -> Initialize 1 device.




Lesson 2.3


Download Workflow

When devices return, download them before making cleaning decisions. A good download log records the date, device identifier, participant identifier, operator, file location, and comments about return status or participant-reported issues.
Recommended ActiLife download steps:

Connect returned devices.
Use Change location to point downloads to the approved raw-data folder.
Select 5-Second Epoch when creating count-based .agd summaries for the ActiLife review workflow.
Click Download all devices.
Check that each expected participant has a file and that file names match the deployment log.
Move files into COMPLETE or INCOMPLETE folders according to the rule below.


Complete / Incomplete Folder Rule

Create two top-level folders inside each download batch:








Folder
Use when…




COMPLETE
The device appears to contain at least half of the planned recording period and can proceed to standard processing/QC.


INCOMPLETE
The device contains less than half of the planned collection time, is missing expected dates, or has a download/device issue that requires review before processing.




The “less than half of planned collection time” rule is an operational triage rule. It does not replace the final valid-day rule of ≥4 days, ≥10 hours of awake wear, and ≥1 weekend day.



Lesson 2.4


Raw Files, Epochs, and Why Both May Appear

Raw data are high-frequency acceleration samples stored by the monitor. For ActiGraph workflows, the raw recording is commonly exported or retained as .gt3x.
An epoch is a fixed time window used to summarize data. A 5-second epoch .agd file is count-based and useful for some ActiLife review workflows. It is not the same as the raw .gt3x file used by GGIR in Module 4.
The distinction matters:









File type
What it represents
Typical role in this course




.gt3x
Raw ActiGraph acceleration data
Main input for GGIR processing.


.agd with 5-second epoch
Count-based ActiLife epoch summary
Useful for ActiLife review, quick checks, and some legacy workflows.


.csv
Open text format exported from raw or processed files
Used only when the downstream pipeline requires it.





Do not mix formats silently: If a report or script uses .agd counts, document that it is epoch/count based. If GGIR processes .gt3x, document the raw-data pipeline separately.


Next Organizing and Converting

After programming and downloading, the next module focuses on organizing, naming, and converting files so the handoff to R/GGIR is reproducible.

title: Accelerometer Introduction
text:
Course home

Module 1


Accelerometer Introduction


Understand what accelerometers record, what they can estimate, why placement matters, and how raw signals become defensible research outcomes.


Concept foundation Placement and protocol Quality-aware workflow






Learning outcomes


By the end of this module, you should be able to:


Explain what an accelerometer records and how raw signals become research outcomes.
Distinguish common accelerometer-derived physical activity, sedentary-behavior, body-posture, activity-type, and sleep-wake measures.
Describe how device placement and protocol choices shape the data that can be collected and interpreted.
Read placement and device tables as planning tools rather than universal rules.



1.1 What is an accelerometer? 1.2 What can accelerometers measure? 1.3 Placement and protocol Knowledge check



Lesson 1.1


What Is an Accelerometer?

An accelerometer is a sensor that records acceleration over time, commonly along three axes. In human movement research, the device does not directly “know” whether a person is exercising, sitting, or asleep. Researchers combine the recorded signal with a documented processing method, a wear location, and a study protocol to derive interpretable outcomes.
The raw signal is commonly stored in gravitational units (g) or metres per second squared (m/s²). Later in the pipeline, the signal can be summarized into metrics such as ENMO (Euclidean Norm Minus One), a raw-acceleration metric that subtracts 1 g to remove the static effect of gravity and summarize dynamic movement. Intensity summaries such as MVPA are not direct sensor readings; they are derived after applying thresholds or algorithms to processed data.


01Plan and deploy

Choose device, placement, sampling, wear protocol, and participant instructions.



02Download

Retrieve raw files and document return status, dates, and operator decisions.



03Organize and convert

Use reproducible folders, IDs, logs, and only convert formats when the pipeline needs it.



04Process

Run GGIR or another approved workflow to calibrate, detect non-wear, and summarize movement.



05Inspect quality

Review reports, plots, missingness, calibration error, clipping, and valid-day status.



06Clean and analyse

Apply documented rules, derive outcomes, prepare the final Stata dataset, and report decisions.




Optional learning activity

In two or three sentences, explain the difference between (a) a raw accelerometer signal, (b) a 5-second epoch file, and (c) an accelerometer-derived physical-activity outcome.





Lesson 1.2


What Can Accelerometers Measure?

Accelerometer recordings can support estimates of several dimensions of movement behavior. The exact outcome depends on the device, placement, algorithm, calibration, and processing choices. Common outcomes include physical-activity volume and intensity, sedentary time, body posture, activity type, and sleep-wake timing.









Domain
Examples of potential outcomes
Interpretation note




Physical activity
Movement volume, energy expenditure, steps, light activity, MVPA, vigorous activity
Estimates are method-dependent; report the processing approach and thresholds.


Sedentary behavior
Sedentary time, breaks, and accumulated sedentary bouts
Posture-sensitive estimates require an appropriate placement and algorithm.


Sleep-wake behavior
Sleep duration, onset, offset, midpoint, latency, efficiency, fragmentation
These are algorithm-derived estimates, not a clinical diagnosis.


Body posture
Sitting, standing, lying
Usually depends on placement such as thigh or trunk sensors.


Activity type
Walking, running, cycling, stair-stepping
Activity-type classification is more algorithm-specific than volume and intensity summaries.






Evidence details: examples of potential accelerometer-derived measures





Category
Indicators
Measurement




Physical activity (Arvidsson et al., 2019)
Energy expenditure
kcal or METs


Physical activity (Arvidsson et al., 2019)
Step count
Study-defined count or daily total


Physical activity (Arvidsson et al., 2019)
Sedentary time
Daily time, minutes, or proportion


Physical activity (Arvidsson et al., 2019)
Light-intensity PA
Daily time, minutes, or proportion


Physical activity (Arvidsson et al., 2019)
Moderate PA
Daily time, minutes, or proportion


Physical activity (Arvidsson et al., 2019)
Vigorous PA
Daily time, minutes, or proportion


Physical activity (Arvidsson et al., 2019)
Moderate-to-vigorous PA (MVPA)
Daily time, minutes, or proportion


Physical activity (Arvidsson et al., 2019)
Total activity counts per day
Count-based summary when the pipeline uses counts


Sleep-wake behaviors (Quante et al., 2015)
Sleep duration
Algorithm-derived sleep interval summary


Sleep-wake behaviors (Quante et al., 2015)
Nap duration
Algorithm-derived nap interval summary


Sleep-wake behaviors (Quante et al., 2015)
Sleep latency
Time from rest/sleep opportunity to estimated sleep onset


Sleep-wake behaviors (Quante et al., 2015)
Sleep maintenance efficiency
Algorithm-derived sleep continuity estimate


Sleep-wake behaviors (Quante et al., 2015)
Sleep fragmentation
Movement/rest pattern during the sleep window


Sleep-wake behaviors (Quante et al., 2015)
Sleep onset, offset, and midpoint
Clock-time estimates derived by the sleep workflow


Body posture with thigh placement (Arvidsson et al., 2019)
Sitting, standing, lying
Daily time, minutes, or proportion


Activity type (Arvidsson et al., 2019)
Walking, running, cycling, stair-stepping
Algorithm-specific activity-type classification








Lesson 1.3


Placement and Protocol Decisions

Before a monitor is initialized, the study team must choose the device model, wear location, start and stop dates, instructions for participants, and criteria for evaluating whether a recording is usable. These decisions affect participant burden and the outcomes that can be validly interpreted.
Different monitor placements are useful for different questions. A thigh-worn monitor may support posture classification, while wrist placement often improves comfort and continuous wear. The best placement follows the research question and protocol rather than a universal rule.



Your browser does not support the video tag. You can download the Lesson 1.3 video from this link.


Lesson 1.3 video: accelerometer introduction and correct wear.



Quick Reference: Placement Planning










Placement
Good fit when the study needs…
Main planning risk




Wrist
High compliance, 24-hour wear, broad activity/sleep workflows
Intensity estimates and cut-points must match wrist placement.


Hip/waist
Ambulatory activity volume and historical comparability
Lower comfort and removal during sleep/bathing may reduce wear time.


Thigh
Posture-sensitive outcomes such as sitting/standing/lying
Attachment method and skin comfort must be planned carefully.


Chest/trunk
Trunk acceleration and some gait/posture applications
More obtrusive; not the default for many free-living studies.


Ankle
Locomotion in infants/toddlers or populations where hip/wrist are limited
Sleep vs sedentary separation may be difficult.


Low back
Balance and some whole-body movement applications
Adherence and belt attachment need protocol attention.






Evidence details: placement strengths and limitations





Placement
Strengths
Limitations




Wrist (Doherty et al., 2017)
Large-scale wrist-worn protocols have been used successfully in population assessment. Participants may wear wrist-worn devices for longer periods than hip-worn devices (Kerr et al., 2017).
Sometimes less accurate than hip placement for selected physical-activity estimates (Kerr et al., 2017).


Chest (Nazarahari & Rouhani, 2018)
Chest-derived acceleration can reflect physical activity intensity and has been used to detect postures and walking modalities (Mukaino et al., 2022; Nazarahari & Rouhani, 2018).
Trunk movement measurement with a chest-mounted accelerometer may be less easy to implement than wrist-worn protocols (Mukaino et al., 2022).


Low back (Williamson et al., 2026)
Commonly used for balance assessment and whole-body movement applications (Ghislieri et al., 2019).
Participant adherence can be challenging in skin-taped or body-worn protocols (Schneller et al., 2017).


Hip (Migueles et al., 2017)
Hip placement has strong historical use and can reflect energy expenditure well in laboratory and locomotive activities (Hildebrand et al., 2014).
Hip placement may be perceived as less comfortable and more socially obtrusive than wrist placement, lowering wear-time compliance in some groups (Scott et al., 2017).


Thigh (Kozey-Keadle et al., 2011)
Thigh inclination can classify sitting more accurately than pelvis inclination, and thigh-based algorithms have been developed for activities such as stair walking and cycling (Peterson et al., 2015; Skotte et al., 2014).
Attachment may require tape solutions that can loosen with activity, clothing changes, or participant discomfort (Arvidsson et al., 2019).


Ankle (Dorris et al., 2025)
Can support continuous 24-hour collection and may capture locomotion when hip or wrist placement is limited (Campbell et al., 2021).
May have difficulty differentiating sedentary time from sleep in young children (Hager et al., 2016).






Device Comparison

Device selection should follow the research question, protocol, planned wear duration, placement, sampling needs, and available analytical workflow. Specifications should always be verified with the manufacturer’s current documentation before procurement or initialization.













Brand
Primary accelerometer
Sample rate
Data storage
Wear location
Water resistance
Battery life




ActiGraph
+/- 8G
30-100 Hertz
180 Days / 4GB
Wrist, waist, ankle, thigh
IPX7
25 days


Axivity
+/-2 / 4 / 8 / 16 g configurable
12.5 - 1600Hz configurable
1024Mb flash non-volatile
Wrist, thigh, waist, or protocol-specific placement
Device/model dependent
7+ days @ 100Hz, 31+ days @ 50Hz


GENEActiv
+/- 8g
Selectable 10-100Hz
0.5Gb non-volatile
Wrist or protocol-specific placement
10m, IP67
30 days @20Hz, 7 days @100Hz


activPAL
+/- 4g
20/50 Hz, 10/25 Hz
64 MB
Thigh
IP65
7 days


Fibion SENS
-4G to 4G
12.5 Hz, optionally 25 Hz
Up to 14 days
Body-worn placement according to protocol
IP68
150 days







Protocol review


Confirm decisions before recording.



Confirm the wear schedule, sampling frequency, valid-day criteria, non-wear algorithm, reporting units, and thresholds with the study’s principal investigator or data lead. Prior cohort protocols are examples, not defaults for every study.



Optional learning activity

For a fictional seven-day study of adults, write a one-paragraph protocol that states the wear location, wear schedule, participant instructions, and quality-review owner.





Wrap-up


Knowledge Check


What is the difference between a raw acceleration signal and an activity outcome?
Why does device placement matter when interpreting accelerometer data?
Name two study decisions that should be documented before devices are deployed.



Suggested answers


The raw signal is a time series recorded by the sensor; an outcome is an estimate derived after processing the signal with documented methods.
Placement changes the movement signal and determines which algorithms and interpretations are appropriate.
Examples include wear location, wear schedule, initialization dates, participant instructions, sampling settings, and valid-data criteria.



Next Programming and Downloading

The next module moves from study design to hands-on fieldwork: programming devices, downloading recordings, and assessing whether collection was complete.

title: Final Dataset in Stata
text:
Course home

Module 7


Final Dataset in Stata


Bring together file organization, GGIR settings, QC exclusions, sleep cleaning, derived bouts, and analysis-ready variables in one reproducible dataset.


Append and merge Reliability Final exclusions






Learning outcomes


By the end of this module, you should be able to:


Describe the full Stata cleaning workflow from GGIR outputs to a final .dta.
Merge person-level, day-level, and night-level files without losing the unit of observation.
Explain why Spearman-Brown Prophecy reliability is useful for accelerometer outcomes.
Save a dated final dataset with reproducible exclusion rules.



7.1 Dataset workflow 7.2 Reliability 7.3 Exclusion rule 7.4 Final outputs



Lesson 7.1


Final-Dataset Workflow

The course cleaning workflow can be written as a numbered Stata plan:

Import part 5 files: import part5_personsummary_WW_..._T... files and preserve the original file names.
Clean ID variables: standardize participant IDs, remove spaces or formatting artifacts, and confirm uniqueness.
Append each lot: append each collection lot/batch into a single person-level dataset while keeping a batch indicator.
Merge person + day files: merge person-level summaries with part2_daysummary or other day-level outputs using the approved ID/date structure.
Clean sleep records: merge or process part4_nightsummary, then drop sleep values coded 2-6 when following the Pelotas/UFPel example rule.
Generate MVPA bouts: create 1-minute, 5-minute, and 10-minute MVPA bout variables plus 20-minute inactivity indicators if GGIR did not automatically create the exact variables needed.
Generate n-days variables: count valid days, weekday/weekend days, and records meeting ≥10 hours awake wear.
Evaluate reliability: apply the Spearman-Brown Prophecy method to estimate how reliability changes with the number of days.
Collapse to wide weekly means: convert from day-level records to participant-level weekly averages or wide-format variables required by the analysis.
Apply three-parameter exclusions: exclude records that fail the approved combination of valid-day count, awake wear duration, and weekend-day requirement.
Save a dated .dta: save the final dataset with a date stamp and export a data dictionary/log.


示例: Pelotas/UFPel cohort The UFPel do-file workflow should be credited to Otavio Leão, Giulia Salaberry, and Charles Lucena. Treat it as a strong reproducible example, not a universal template: your study must decide its own ID cleaning, valid-day rules, bout definitions, and exclusion criteria.




Lesson 7.2


Spearman-Brown Prophecy Reliability

The Spearman-Brown Prophecy method estimates how reliability changes when the number of measurement days changes. In accelerometer studies, this is useful because a single day may be noisy, while multiple valid days better approximate habitual activity.
The transferable idea is:

estimate reliability for the current number of valid days,
project reliability if more or fewer days are used,
use the result to justify minimum valid-day rules,
report the rule transparently in the methods section.

This is a valuable method to teach because it connects data cleaning with measurement quality rather than treating valid days as an arbitrary cutoff.



Lesson 7.3


Three-Parameter Exclusion Rule

For the course example, final inclusion is evaluated using three parameters:




Parameter
Example rule




Number of valid days
≥4 valid days


Awake wear duration
≥10 hours awake wear per valid day


Weekend coverage
At least 1 weekend day




Keep the exclusion log. It should report how many participants were excluded by each rule, how many failed multiple rules, and the final analytic sample.

Critical review point: Do not define validity rules, aggregation procedures, or outcome thresholds only after seeing results. The data-management and analysis plans should guide these decisions.




Lesson 7.4


Save Final Outputs

Final outputs should include:

dated .dta file,
data dictionary,
cleaning log,
exclusion table,
Stata do-file,
README with source GGIR run and batch dates.

Example file name:
accelerometer_final_analysis_YYYYMMDD.dta

Video 7.1 — Building the final Stata dataset (target: 10–12 minutes) Walk through the numbered workflow, especially ID cleaning, append/merge logic, sleep-code exclusion, MVPA bout creation, Spearman-Brown reliability, and final exclusions.


Next Knowledge Checking

The final dataset should be reproducible, dated, and explainable. A collaborator should be able to open the do-file and understand exactly how raw GGIR outputs became analysis-ready variables.

title: Checking Data Quality
text:
Course home

Module 5


Checking Data Quality


Use logs, summaries, plots, and protocol rules to decide whether processed accelerometer files are trustworthy enough to clean and analyze.


QC outputs Calibration and clipping Valid-day review






Learning outcomes


By the end of this module, you should be able to:


Locate the main GGIR QC outputs.
Explain clipping_score, calib_err, and complete_24hcycle.
Distinguish QC visual inspection files from participant-facing reports.
Document quality decisions before final cleaning.



5.1 Locate QC outputs 5.2 Read QC variables 5.3 Visual inspection 5.4 Valid-day review



Lesson 5.1


Locate Real QC Outputs

After GGIR processing, inspect the output folder before opening Stata. The exact folder structure can vary by GGIR version and options, but the following outputs should be explicitly checked when present:








Output
What to look for




results/QC/data_quality_report.csv
Summary indicators for calibration, clipping, missingness, and recording quality.


Per-file .png quality plots
Visual patterns that reveal odd days, missing segments, non-wear blocks, or device problems.


PDF visualizations
Higher-level plots useful for review, communication, or documentation.


Time_report_... files and related .png plots
QC-oriented visual inspection of the wear pattern and daily signal.





Important correction: The visualreport produced around part 5 is mainly participant-facing / communication-oriented. For QC, rely on Time_report_... files, .png quality plots, and QC summaries rather than treating the part 5 visual report as the primary QC tool.




Lesson 5.2


Read the QC Variables

Three variables deserve special attention because they often appear in quizzes or downstream filters:









Variable
Meaning
How to use it




clipping_score
Indicator of whether the acceleration signal hits the sensor range, suggesting signal saturation or clipping.
High or unusual clipping requires review before trusting intensity summaries.


calib_err
Calibration error, often interpreted in mg (milligravity units).
Large calibration error can indicate poor calibration or device/signal problems; compare to the study’s approved threshold.


complete_24hcycle
Whether a recording/day contains a complete 24-hour cycle according to the processing definition.
Useful for identifying days with complete day-night coverage, especially in 24-hour protocols.




These variables should be taught before they appear in Module 8. Learners should know both the variable name and the practical decision it supports.



Lesson 5.3


Visual Inspection

Numerical summaries can miss problems that are obvious in plots. During visual inspection, look for:

flat lines suggesting non-wear, device malfunction, or removal,
abrupt shifts that suggest device handling or placement change,
unexpectedly short or long recordings,
missing day-night cycles,
calibration or clipping warnings,
disagreement between logs and processed output.

Visual QC should be reproducible. Keep a QC log with:

participant ID,
file name,
date reviewed,
reviewer,
concern category,
action taken,
whether a second reviewer is needed.




Lesson 5.4


Valid-Day Review

A non-wear interval is a period when the device is likely not being worn. A valid day is a day that meets the study’s minimum usable-data criteria. For the course example, valid-day review uses:

at least 4 valid days,
at least 10 hours of awake wear per valid day,
at least 1 weekend day.

Do not apply these rules silently in Stata. First confirm the GGIR outputs contain the variables needed to evaluate them and document any exceptions.

Video 5.1 — Reviewing QC output (target: 8–10 minutes) Show results/QC/data_quality_report.csv, a per-file .png plot, a Time_report_... file, and one PDF visualization. Walk through a clear keep/review/exclude decision.


Next Cleaning and Standardizing

QC turns raw processing output into defensible data-management decisions. The next module shows how to clean and standardize GGIR output files after QC concerns have been documented.

title: Accelerometer Learning Course
text:
University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign / LA PAISTA Lab


Accelerometer Learning Course


Turn raw movement signals into reproducible evidence.


A practical online course for planning accelerometer studies, processing raw movement files, checking quality, and building clean analysis-ready datasets.


Start Module 1 View modules





8applied modules


3core tools: ActiLife, GGIR, Stata


1end-to-end reproducible workflow





Course overview


Built for research teams who need clean accelerometer data.


Accelerometry turns continuous movement signals into information about activity, sedentary behavior, and sleep. This course follows the practical decisions that make that transformation credible: device setup, file organization, processing, quality control, cleaning, and documentation.


The examples use ActiGraph, GGIR in R, and Stata. The principles are broader than any one device or software package, and every study should document its own protocol and analytical decisions.





Your browser does not support the video tag. You can download the welcome video from this link.


Welcome video for the Accelerometer Learning Course.






Workflow


From device deployment to analysis-ready evidence.



01Collect

Initialize devices, define placement and wear protocol, and support participant compliance.



02Download

Retrieve raw recordings and document return status, dates, and operator decisions.



03Organize

Use reproducible folders, IDs, file naming, conversion logs, and transparent storage.



04Process

Run GGIR or another approved workflow to calibrate, detect non-wear, and summarize movement.



05Inspect

Review plots, missingness, calibration, clipping, valid days, and unusual movement patterns.



06Deliver

Clean, standardize, derive outcomes, prepare Stata datasets, and report analytical decisions.









Course map


Modules designed around the real research workflow.


Begin with Module 1



Module 1Accelerometer IntroductionWhat accelerometers record, what they can estimate, and how placement and protocol shape interpretation.


Module 2Programming and DownloadingConfigure monitors, download recordings, and assess whether collection was complete.


Module 3Organizing and ConvertingCreate reproducible folders, file names, batches, and conversion logs.


Module 4Setting Up R and GGIRPrepare R/RStudio, define inputs and outputs, and understand the GGIR workflow.


Module 5Checking Data QualityReview summaries and visual patterns; document concerns and decisions.


Module 6Cleaning and StandardizingClean GGIR outputs, standardize variables, and preserve a transparent data trail.


Module 7Final Dataset in StataImport, append, verify, and prepare the final analysis dataset.


Module 8Knowledge CheckingConsolidate the workflow and test key decisions before using study data.





Study aidGlossaryKey accelerometer, GGIR, QC, and Stata terms used across the course.


Evidence baseReferencesBibliographic sources and software documentation cited in the modules.





Protocol standard


Every parameter needs a study reason.



Device configuration, valid-day rules, wear-time criteria, thresholds, and processing arguments must be selected for the study population, device, wear location, and protocol. This course teaches the decision process; it does not prescribe one universal parameter set.

title: Setting Up R and GGIR
text:
Course home

Module 4


Setting Up R and GGIR


Prepare an auditable R/GGIR workspace and learn how one documented GGIR() call turns raw files into quality-control, activity, and sleep outputs.


R/RStudio setup GGIR parts 1-6 Processing parameters






Learning outcomes


By the end of this module, you should be able to:


Prepare R/RStudio and a project-specific GGIR workspace.
Describe GGIR’s six numbered processing/reporting parts.
Write a documented GGIR() call using current parameter names.
Explain what each major parameter controls before running a study batch.



4.1 Software environment 4.2 GGIR parts 4.3 Core parameters 4.4 Preserve the run



Lesson 4.1


Install and Document the Software Environment

GGIR is an R package that processes raw accelerometer recordings into summaries that can support physical-activity, sedentary-behavior, and sleep-wake analyses. The current workflow should be taught as a single GGIR() function call with documented arguments, not as separate calls to older internal functions.
Official software links:

R: https://cran.r-project.org/
RStudio: https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/
GGIR CRAN page/manual: https://cran.r-project.org/package=GGIR

Install GGIR in R:
install.packages("GGIR")
Before running GGIR, record:

R version,
GGIR version,
time zone,
device type and placement,
input folder,
output folder,
operator,
date of processing,
approved protocol parameters.


示例: Pelotas/UFPel cohort Use a dedicated project folder with separate subfolders for raw .gt3x files, GGIR output, QC review, and Stata files. Keep the exact GGIR() call used for each processing run in a dated script, even when only one argument changes.




Lesson 4.2


GGIR’s Six Numbered Parts

GGIR documentation lists six numbered parts. For this course, the core individual-level pipeline usually relies on parts 1-5, while part 6 is optional and should be used only when the analysis plan needs it. Still, we refer to the workflow as six numbered parts so learners can recognize current GGIR terminology.









Part
Plain-language role
Course interpretation




Part 1
Read raw files, perform calibration, detect clipping, derive raw metrics such as ENMO
Creates the foundation for all later summaries.


Part 2
Summarize data quality, non-wear, and day-level information
Main place to review valid days and data-quality flags.


Part 3
Detect sustained inactivity and sleep-window candidates when sleep analysis is requested
Bridges raw movement into sleep-period estimation.


Part 4
Summarize sleep and night-level outputs
Produces night summaries used in sleep cleaning.


Part 5
Create person-level summaries and selected reports
Produces files such as part5_personsummary_...; reports can be useful but are not the same as QC plots.


Part 6
Optional/co-analysis/reporting functionality listed in current documentation
Use only when the study plan needs this functionality.





Terminology update: Teach data_masking_strategy, not the older shorthand strategy. Teach GGIR() as the single user-facing function call.




Lesson 4.3


Core GGIR Parameters

The values below are examples for teaching. They are not universal defaults.
library(GGIR)

GGIR(
  datadir = "03_ggir_input",
  outputdir = "04_ggir_output",
  mode = 1:6,
  do.enmo = TRUE,
  data_masking_strategy = 1,
  hrs.del.start = 0,
  maxdur = 0,
  qwindow = c(0, 24),
  mvpathreshold = c(100, 120),
  timethreshold = 5,
  anglethreshold = 3,
  def.noc.sleep = 1,
  threshold.lig = 30,
  threshold.mod = 100,
  threshold.vig = 400,
  timewindow = c("MM", "WW")
)









Parameter
What it controls
Decision to document




datadir
Folder containing input accelerometer files, usually raw .gt3x for this course.
Which batch/folder was processed.


outputdir
Folder where GGIR writes results and reports.
Where outputs are archived and reviewed.


mode
Which GGIR parts to run.
Whether the run covers all six numbered parts or a selected subset.


do.enmo
Whether to calculate ENMO, the raw acceleration metric used in many GGIR workflows.
Which acceleration metric is reported.


data_masking_strategy
How GGIR masks or handles data windows for waking/sleep periods.
Why the selected strategy fits the protocol.


hrs.del.start
Hours removed from the start of each recording.
Whether deployment artifacts or first-day behavior should be excluded.


maxdur
Maximum duration processed from each file; 0 lets GGIR use available data.
Whether processing should be truncated to a study window.


qwindow
Time window used for day-level summaries.
Whether analysis uses 24-hour windows or a narrower period.


mvpathreshold
Thresholds for MVPA-related summaries.
Which published or study-specific threshold supports the outcome.


timethreshold
Time threshold embedded in summary file names, such as T5.
Minimum duration logic for time-based summaries.


anglethreshold
Angle threshold embedded in summary file names, such as A3.
Why this angle rule is appropriate for the protocol.


def.noc.sleep
How GGIR defines nights/sleep windows when diary information is unavailable or incomplete.
Whether diaries, algorithms, or default rules are used.


threshold.lig, threshold.mod, threshold.vig
Light, moderate, and vigorous cut-points used in intensity summaries.
The cut-point set and population/placement justification.


timewindow
Summary windows such as MM and WW.
Which windows are required for reporting and Stata cleaning.







Lesson 4.4


Process Raw Files, Then Preserve the Run

After running GGIR, do not immediately delete intermediate folders. Save:

the processing script,
package/session information,
the raw input list,
output folders,
warnings/errors,
the date and operator.


Video 4.1 — A guided tour of the GGIR workflow (target: 8–10 minutes) Show the single GGIR() call, explain each argument in the table above, then point to the output folders used in Modules 5 and 6.


Next Checking Data Quality

The goal is not to memorize one parameter set. The goal is to know what each parameter does and how to document the study decision before processing begins.

title: Cleaning and Standardizing
text:
Course home

Module 6


Cleaning and Standardizing


Turn many GGIR output files into a documented set of variables that can be merged, filtered, analyzed, and audited consistently.


Output files Sleep cleaning Data dictionary






Learning outcomes


By the end of this module, you should be able to:


Identify the main GGIR output files used for day, night, and person-level cleaning.
Decode common GGIR part 5 file names.
Apply sleep-cleaning rules and understand why MVPA bouts may need to be generated outside GGIR.
Build a data dictionary that links variables back to source files.



6.1 GGIR output files 6.2 Decode file names 6.3 Sleep cleaning 6.4 MVPA bouts 6.5 Standardize variables



Lesson 6.1


Know the Real GGIR Output Files

The exact output names can vary by GGIR version and options. In the course workflow, learners should recognize these common files:









Output file pattern
Unit of observation
Typical use




part2_summary
Participant/file
Overall recording, calibration, and data-quality summaries.


part2_daysummary
Participant-day
Day-level wear, activity, and quality information.


part4_nightsummary
Participant-night
Sleep/night summaries used for sleep cleaning.


part5_personsummary_WW_..._T...
Participant-level summary
Person-level outcomes for selected time windows and thresholds.




Before cleaning, confirm the unit of observation. A common mistake is merging a day-level file to a person-level file without preserving day identifiers.

示例: Pelotas/UFPel cohort Treat GGIR outputs as structured evidence: keep part 2, part 4, and part 5 files in their original output folders; copy only the files needed for cleaning into the Stata working folder. Keep a record of which lot or processing batch each file came from.




Lesson 6.2


Decode GGIR File Names

GGIR file names often encode the cut-points and thresholds used. For example:
part5_personsummary_WW_L30M200V700_T5A3.csv
Read this as:




Segment
Meaning




part5_personsummary
Participant-level summary from part 5.


WW
Time window or day-window summary label.


L30
Light-intensity threshold/cut-point = 30.


M200
Moderate-intensity threshold/cut-point = 200.


V700
Vigorous-intensity threshold/cut-point = 700.


T5
timethreshold = 5.


A3
anglethreshold = 3.




The values in a file name are not decoration; they are part of the analysis method. If two files differ only in L/M/V, T, or A, they are different processing outputs.



Lesson 6.3


Sleep Cleaning

Sleep outputs often include codes that need to be reviewed before analysis. In the course example, sleep values coded 2-6 are dropped during cleaning. The exact meaning of each code should be documented from the study’s GGIR output/data dictionary before exclusion.
Minimum sleep-cleaning steps:

Import part4_nightsummary.
Confirm participant ID and night/date variables.
Review the sleep-status or quality code used by the study.
Drop values coded 2-6 when this is the approved Pelotas/UFPel rule.
Save a cleaned night-level file and document how many records were removed.


Study-specific rule: Dropping sleep values 2-6 is an example rule from the course pipeline. Your study should confirm which codes represent unusable or non-target sleep records before applying the same filter.




Lesson 6.4


MVPA Bouts Not Automatically Produced by GGIR

GGIR can produce many activity summaries, but some bout variables may need to be generated in Stata or another downstream tool. In this course example, the Stata cleaning workflow creates:

1-minute MVPA bouts,
5-minute MVPA bouts,
10-minute MVPA bouts,
20-minute inactivity variables.

These variables require a clear definition of what counts as a bout, how interruptions are handled, and whether the rule uses raw acceleration metrics, epoch counts, or derived intensity classifications.



Lesson 6.5


Standardize Variable Names

Build a data dictionary that states:

original variable name,
standardized variable name,
source file,
unit of observation,
unit of measurement,
transformation,
missing-value rule,
reason for exclusion or retention.


Video 6.1 — Reading and cleaning GGIR outputs (target: 8–10 minutes) Show the source files, decode one part5_personsummary_WW_..._T... name, then demonstrate sleep-code exclusion and MVPA bout generation at a conceptual level.


Next Final Dataset in Stata

Cleaning should preserve traceability. Every standardized variable should point back to a source file and a processing decision.

title: Glossary
text:
Course home

Study aid


Glossary


Key terms used across the accelerometer course, with definitions that study teams can adapt to their own protocol, device, and analysis plan.


Terminology QC variables Study rules






Accelerometer

A sensor that records acceleration over time, commonly along three axes.



ActiLife

ActiGraph desktop software used for device initialization, download, and selected conversion/review workflows.



Calibration error (calib_err)

A measure of how well the accelerometer signal can be calibrated, often interpreted in milligravity units (mg). Large values require review.



Clipping score (clipping_score)

A signal-quality indicator showing whether acceleration values hit the sensor range, suggesting saturation or clipping.



Complete 24-hour cycle (complete_24hcycle)

Indicator that a recording/day contains a complete 24-hour cycle according to the processing definition.



ENMO

Euclidean Norm Minus One; a raw-acceleration metric that summarizes dynamic acceleration after subtracting the static effect of gravity.



Epoch

A fixed time window, such as 5 seconds, used to summarize raw acceleration into counts or other aggregate values.



.agd

ActiGraph count-based data file; in this course, a 5-second epoch .agd is distinct from raw .gt3x data.



.gt3x

ActiGraph raw-data file commonly used as GGIR input.



GGIR

R package used to process raw accelerometer data into quality-control, activity, and sleep-related outputs.



MET

Metabolic equivalent; a unit used to express energy expenditure relative to rest.



MVPA

Moderate-to-vigorous physical activity.



Non-wear

A period when the device is likely not being worn by the participant.



Pelotas/UFPel cohort example

Course example used to illustrate real operations. It is not a universal rule set.



QC

Quality control; the process of reviewing logs, summaries, plots, and protocol rules to decide whether data are trustworthy.



Raw data

High-frequency sensor measurements before epoch aggregation or derived-outcome processing.



Time window MM / WW

GGIR summary-window labels used in output file names. The exact interpretation should be documented from the study’s GGIR run and output dictionary.



Valid day

A day that meets the study’s minimum usable-data rule. In the course example: ≥10 hours awake wear and used as part of the ≥4 valid days plus ≥1 weekend day rule.



Wear protocol

The study’s instructions for when, where, and how participants wear the device.




Evidence base References

Review the studies and software documentation cited throughout the course.

title: Knowledge Checking
text:
Course home

Module 8


Knowledge Checking


Check whether you can apply the course workflow, not just remember vocabulary, before using real study data.


File types QC decisions Study rules






Learning outcomes


By the end of this module, you should be able to:


Identify the correct file type for each processing step.
Choose the appropriate QC output for a review task.
Match GGIR parameters and output names to their meanings.
Apply valid-day and cleaning rules to short examples.



Quick checks Applied questions Short-answer review Final reflection



Self-check


Quick Interactive Checks




Question 1: GGIR processing in this course primarily uses which ActiGraph file type?


Answer: .gt3x raw file.




Question 2: A 5-second .agd file is best described as what?


Answer: A count-based epoch summary.




Question 3: ENMO stands for what?


Answer: Euclidean Norm Minus One.




Question 4: MVPA means what?


Answer: Moderate-to-vigorous physical activity.




Question 5: Which current GGIR parameter name should be taught?


Answer: data_masking_strategy.




Question 6: The GGIR documentation lists six numbered parts. True or false?


Answer: True.




Question 7: In the course example, a valid participant record requires at least 4 valid days, ≥10 hours awake wear, and at least 1 weekend day. True or false?


Answer: True.




Question 8: The part 5 visualreport should be treated as the primary QC tool. True or false?


Answer: False. It is mainly participant-facing / communication-oriented; QC should use Time_report_... files, .png plots, and QC summaries when present.




Question 9: The QC variable calib_err is a calibration error commonly interpreted in mg. True or false?


Answer: True.




Question 10: An incorrect consumer-device row with a non-real product name should be retained in the device table. True or false?


Answer: False. Incorrect device labels should be removed or corrected rather than taught.






Application


Applied Questions




Scenario 1: A participant was scheduled for 7 days of recording, but the returned monitor contains less than 3 days of usable collection time. Which first-pass folder should it go into?


Answer: INCOMPLETE.


Less than half of the planned collection period should be triaged as INCOMPLETE. This is an operational folder rule; final valid-day rules are evaluated later after processing.




Scenario 2: In part5_personsummary_WW_L30M200V700_T5A3.csv, what does M200 represent?


Answer: Moderate-intensity threshold/cut-point = 200.


L30M200V700 encodes light, moderate, and vigorous cut-points. T5 and A3 encode the time and angle thresholds.




Scenario 3: You want to inspect calibration, clipping, missingness, and recording-quality summaries. Which file is the best starting point when present?


Answer: results/QC/data_quality_report.csv.




Scenario 4: In the Pelotas/UFPel example, sleep values coded 2-6 are dropped during cleaning. Is this a universal rule for every accelerometer study?


Answer: No. It is an example rule that must be verified for each study.




Scenario 5: Why is the Spearman-Brown Prophecy method useful in this course?


Answer: It estimates how reliability changes with the number of measurement days.






Review


Short-Answer Review

Write short answers to these prompts, then compare with the suggested answers.

What is the difference between a raw signal and an activity outcome?
Why does placement affect interpretation?
Why should conversion from .gt3x to .csv be described as pipeline-dependent?
What is the difference between a participant-level file and a day-level file?
Why should exclusion rules be decided before examining study associations?



Suggested answers


A raw signal is the sensor’s recorded acceleration time series; an outcome is a processed estimate derived using documented methods.
Placement changes what motion is captured and determines which algorithms, thresholds, and interpretations are appropriate.
GGIR can read .gt3x directly, so CSV conversion is needed only if a downstream pipeline requires it.
Participant-level files summarize one person; day-level files contain separate records for each participant-day.
Pre-specified rules reduce bias and make the analytic sample defensible.





Final reflection


Before Using Real Study Data

You should be able to explain:

which file type is used at each stage,
where QC outputs live,
what the main GGIR parameters mean,
how valid days and sleep records are cleaned,
how the final Stata dataset is assembled,
where each decision is documented.

These questions were reviewed by the course team and aligned to the current course modules.

Continue Glossary

Use the glossary to review the key accelerometer, GGIR, QC, and Stata terms that appear across the course.

title: Organizing and Converting
text:
Course home

Module 3


Organizing and Converting


Create a file system that makes every accelerometer file traceable to the participant, batch, software setting, and processing decision that produced it.


Folder design Batch logs Conversion decisions






Learning outcomes


By the end of this module, you should be able to:


Build a transparent folder structure for raw, converted, processed, and final files.
Apply consistent file-naming rules and batch logs.
Convert ActiGraph files in ActiLife when conversion is needed.
Explain why .gt3x to .csv conversion is pipeline-dependent rather than universally required.



3.1 Folder structure 3.2 Naming and batch logs 3.3 ActiLife conversion 3.4 Is conversion necessary?



Lesson 3.1


Folder Structure

Use a folder structure that separates raw evidence from files created later in the pipeline. A simple structure is:
accelerometer_project/
  01_raw_downloads/
    batch_001/
      COMPLETE/
      INCOMPLETE/
  02_converted_files/
  03_ggir_input/
  04_ggir_output/
  05_qc_review/
  06_cleaned_data/
  07_stata_final/
  logs/
Raw downloads should be treated as read-only after the first handoff. If a file must be renamed, keep a log linking the original file name, new study ID, device ID, download batch, and operator.

示例: Pelotas/UFPel cohort Separate each collection lot or batch before processing. Keep the original raw downloads, the conversion outputs, and GGIR outputs in different folders. This makes it possible to append lots later in Stata without losing the batch history.




Lesson 3.2


Naming and Batch Logs

Every file should be traceable to:

participant or study ID,
device serial number,
collection lot/batch,
download date,
operator,
file format and processing status.

Avoid names such as participant1.csv or newfile.gt3x. Prefer a naming pattern such as:
studyid_batch_deviceid_YYYYMMDD_raw.gt3x
studyid_batch_deviceid_YYYYMMDD_converted.csv
The exact pattern can differ across studies; the important rule is that another analyst can reconstruct where each file came from.



Lesson 3.3


ActiLife Conversion Workflow

Some workflows convert ActiGraph files to .csv for software that cannot read .gt3x. If conversion is part of the approved workflow, use a batch process rather than manual one-by-one exports.
Recommended ActiLife conversion steps:

Open ActiLife.
Go to File -> Import/Export/Convert.
Choose RAW to RAW.
Select GT3X -> CSV (batch).
Select the input folder containing .gt3x files.
Uncheck TimeStamps if the downstream workflow expects relative time rather than a full timestamp column.
Check create subfolder so converted files are separated from the originals.
Run the batch conversion and save the conversion log.


Video 3.1 — Batch conversion in ActiLife (target: 5–7 minutes) Demonstrate: File -> Import/Export/Convert -> RAW to RAW -> GT3X->CSV(batch) -> uncheck TimeStamps -> check create subfolder.




Lesson 3.4


Is Conversion Always Necessary?

No. GGIR can directly read .gt3x files, so converting .gt3x to .csv is not required for the GGIR pipeline taught in Module 4. Conversion is useful only when a downstream script, external software package, data-sharing requirement, or audit workflow specifically needs .csv.

Pipeline decision: Do not teach conversion as a mandatory step. Write, “Convert only if your pipeline requires CSV; otherwise keep .gt3x as the GGIR input.”

If a batch begins with 100 .gt3x files and all convert successfully, you should expect 100 converted output files, not 200 files becoming 100 files. If the counts differ, stop and reconcile the conversion log before processing.

Next Setting Up R and GGIR

Before processing begins, verify that each input file has a corresponding study identifier, source location, and batch record.
